options: Rupture of membranes after the onset of labor
Spontaneous rupture of fetal membranes before the onset of labor
Rupture of membranes at full dilatation
Artificial rupture of membranes during induction
s2.explain: PROM is a spontaneous rupture of the fetal membranes before the onset of labor.

s2.q: Preterm PROM (PPROM) is defined as rupture of membranes occurring:
s2.explain: Preterm PROM (PPROM) is spontaneous rupture of membranes before 37 completed weeks of gestation.

s2.q: The key feature that distinguishes PROM from normal rupture of membranes is:
options: Rupture occurring before the onset of labor
s2.explain: The phrase 'before the onset of labor' is what distinguishes PROM from the normal rupture of membranes that occurs during labor.

s2.q: Which of the following is a key concept?
options: It is essential for understanding the condition
It is rarely relevant in clinical practice
It applies only to specific populations
It has been replaced by newer classifications
s2.explain: Understanding key concepts is fundamental to clinical management.

s2.q: When assessing this aspect, the most important factor is:
s2.explain: Assessment requires integration of clinical, laboratory, and imaging findings.

s3.q: Which of the following is the most clinically important cause of PROM?
s3.explain: Intrauterine infection (chorioamnionitis, subclinical deciduitis) is the most clinically important cause — infection weakens the chorioamniotic membranes and predisposes them to rupture.

s3.q: Which of the following is NOT one of the 5 classic etiologies of PROM?
s3.explain: The 5 classic etiologies are: intrauterine infection, prior history of PPROM, trauma, amniocentesis, and polyhydramnios.

s3.q: Polyhydramnios causes PROM through which mechanism?
options: Direct damage to fetal membranes by inflammation
Mechanical over-distension of the uterus increasing membrane stress
s3.explain: Polyhydramnios causes PROM by mechanically over-distending the uterus, increasing wall tension and membrane stress.

s3.q: The clinical significance of this section is:
options: It applies only to research settings
s3.explain: Understanding these concepts directly informs clinical management.

s3.q: The etiology of this condition involves:
options: A complex interplay of genetic, environmental, and multifactorial elements
s3.explain: Most obstetric conditions have multifactorial etiology.

s4.q: The typical presentation of PROM is:
options: Gradual leaking of thick white discharge
Sudden gush of clear or pale yellow fluid from vagina
Painful uterine contractions with heavy bleeding
s4.explain: Patients present with a typical history of sudden gush of clear or pale yellow fluid leaking from the vagina.

s4.q: Meconium-stained (green/brown) fluid in suspected PROM suggests:
s4.explain: Meconium-stained (green/brown) fluid suggests fetal distress, while foul-smelling fluid suggests chorioamnionitis.

s4.q: A history of a sudden, single, large gush of fluid followed by continued leakage is characteristic of:
s4.explain: A history of a sudden, single, large gush followed by continued leakage distinguishes PROM from urinary incontinence (continuous, no gush) or excessive vaginal discharge (gradual, no gush).

s4.q: The clinical significance of this section is:
options: It applies only to research settings
s4.explain: Understanding these concepts directly informs clinical management.

s4.q: Which of the following is a key concept?
options: It is essential for understanding the condition
It is rarely relevant in clinical practice
It applies only to specific populations
It has been replaced by newer classifications
s4.explain: Understanding key concepts is fundamental to clinical management.

s5.q: What is the first step in the diagnosis of PROM?
s5.explain: Speculum examination is the first step in the diagnosis of PROM — it is used to directly demonstrate leaking of fluid from the cervical os.

s5.q: The nitrazine test for PROM is based on which principle?
options: pH difference: amniotic fluid (7–7.5) vs vaginal discharge (3.5–4.5)
s5.explain: The pH of amniotic fluid is 7–7.5 (alkaline) while vaginal discharge pH is 3.5–4.5 (acidic). Yellow nitrazine paper turning blue indicates alkaline amniotic fluid.

s5.q: A positive fern test for PROM appears as:
options: A blue coloration on the slide
Fern pattern (arborization) under microscopy after slide dries
Crystal formation at the bottom of the tube
s5.explain: Fluid from the posterior vaginal fornix is swabbed on a glass slide and allowed to dry for 10 minutes. If a fern pattern (arborization) appears, amniotic fluid is present.

s5.q: Fetal fibronectin is detected in what percentage of females with PROM?
s5.explain: Fetal fibronectin can be detected in only 39% of females with PROM by means of an ELISA test. Its sensitivity is limited.

s5.q: In obstetric practice, this knowledge helps:
s5.explain: Comprehensive knowledge enables prevention, early diagnosis, and optimal management.

s6.q: Which of the following is NOT one of the 4 effects of preterm PROM?
s6.explain: The 4 effects of preterm PROM are: preterm labor, pulmonary hypoplasia, skeletal and joint deformities, and chorioamnionitis. Fetal macrosomia is associated with postterm pregnancy, not PROM.

s6.q: Pulmonary hypoplasia from PROM results from:
options: Direct infection of fetal lung tissue
Premature closure of the ductus arteriosus
s6.explain: Pulmonary hypoplasia is due to severe oligohydramnios — the fetus needs amniotic fluid for normal lung development; severe fluid loss impairs alveolar formation.

s6.q: Why is pulmonary hypoplasia from prolonged PPROM considered irreversible?
options: It responds only to surfactant therapy
The lungs never develop adequately after birth once alveolar formation is impaired in utero
It requires fetal surgery to correct
s6.explain: Pulmonary hypoplasia from prolonged severe oligohydramnios is not reversible after birth. This is why very early PPROM (< 24 weeks) carries very high neonatal mortality.

s6.q: In obstetric practice, this knowledge helps:
s6.explain: Comprehensive knowledge enables prevention, early diagnosis, and optimal management.

s6.q: When assessing this aspect, the most important factor is:
s6.explain: Assessment requires integration of clinical, laboratory, and imaging findings.

s7.q: General management of PROM includes all EXCEPT:
options: Monitoring for signs of meconium staining or infection
s7.explain: General management includes bed rest and sterile pads for inspection for meconium staining, foul smell (infection), or blood. Immediate induction depends on gestational age.

s7.q: Sterile pads in PROM management are used to inspect for:
options: Meconium staining, foul smell (chorioamnionitis), and blood
s7.explain: Sterile pads are inspected for: meconium staining (greenish-brown — fetal distress), foul smell (chorioamnionitis), and blood (abruption or vasa previa).

s7.q: Greenish-brown staining on sterile pads in a PROM patient suggests:
s7.explain: Greenish-brown staining indicates meconium staining, which suggests fetal distress.

s7.q: Which factor most influences the choice of treatment?
options: All of the above — treatment is individualized
s7.explain: Obstetric management is tailored to gestational age, maternal status, and available resources.

s7.q: When assessing this aspect, the most important factor is:
s7.explain: Assessment requires integration of clinical, laboratory, and imaging findings.

s8.q: The recommended management for PROM occurring before 26 weeks is:
s8.explain: Before 26 weeks, the risk of developing chorioamnionitis is very high, so termination of pregnancy is recommended.

s8.q: Why is termination recommended for PROM before 26 weeks?
options: Very high chorioamnionitis risk and pulmonary hypoplasia is likely
Maternal risk of hemorrhage is too high
Fetus is too large for vaginal delivery
s8.explain: Reasons include very high chorioamnionitis risk, likely pulmonary hypoplasia from oligohydramnios, likely skeletal deformities (Potter sequence), and fetal viability below 24-26 weeks.

options: Compression-related skeletal deformities from prolonged severe oligohydramnios
s8.explain: Compression-related deformities such as clubfoot from prolonged severe oligohydramnios are known as Potter sequence.

s8.q: When assessing this aspect, the most important factor is:
s8.explain: Assessment requires integration of clinical, laboratory, and imaging findings.

s8.q: The clinical significance of this section is:
options: It applies only to research settings
s8.explain: Understanding these concepts directly informs clinical management.

s10.q: For PROM occurring after 34 weeks, the recommended management is:
s10.explain: After 34 weeks, the fetus is sufficiently mature that the risk of conservative management (chorioamnionitis, cord prolapse) outweighs the risk of prematurity. Delivery is indicated.

s10.q: Why is the cutoff for PROM management set at 34 weeks?
options: Fetal skull is fully ossified by 34 weeks
By 34 weeks, most fetuses produce enough endogenous surfactant, and risk of ongoing oligohydramnios dominates
Cervical ripening is always successful after 34 weeks
s10.explain: By 34 weeks, most fetuses have produced enough endogenous surfactant that additional benefit from corticosteroids is minimal, and the risk of ongoing oligohydramnios + ascending infection becomes the dominant clinical concern.

s10.q: The 34-week cutoff in PROM management is the same as in which other obstetric condition management?
s10.explain: The 34-week cutoff is the same as in the management of preterm labor (Chapter 31) and is anchored on lung-maturation data.

s10.q: Which factor most influences the choice of treatment?
options: All of the above — treatment is individualized
s10.explain: Obstetric management is tailored to gestational age, maternal status, and available resources.

s10.q: When assessing this aspect, the most important factor is:
s10.explain: Assessment requires integration of clinical, laboratory, and imaging findings.

s11.explain: Chorioamnionitis is defined as inflammation of fetal membranes, which means intrauterine infection.

s11.q: The diagnosis of chorioamnionitis requires fever > 38°C plus at least one of how many signs/investigations?
s11.explain: The diagnosis is made when fever > 38°C is present PLUS at least one of the 7 signs: maternal tachycardia, fetal tachycardia, uterine tenderness, foul smelling amniotic fluid, maternal leukocytosis > 16,000/cc, raised CRP > 2.5, or positive high vaginal swab.

s11.q: Which of the following is a diagnostic sign of chorioamnionitis?
s11.explain: Maternal leukocytosis > 16,000/cc is one of the 7 diagnostic signs of chorioamnionitis (along with fever > 38°C).

s11.q: Which of the following is a key concept?
options: It is essential for understanding the condition
It is rarely relevant in clinical practice
It applies only to specific populations
It has been replaced by newer classifications
s11.explain: Understanding key concepts is fundamental to clinical management.

s11.q: The clinical significance of this section is:
options: It applies only to research settings
s11.explain: Understanding these concepts directly informs clinical management.

s12.q: The definitive treatment for chorioamnionitis is:
options: Delivery and evacuation of uterine contents
s12.explain: The definitive treatment is delivery and evacuation of the uterine contents. Antibiotics alone are not curative while the infected focus (amniotic cavity) remains in place.

s12.q: The antibiotic regimen for chorioamnionitis until delivery is:
options: Ampicillin 2 g IV q6h + Gentamicin (2 mg/kg load, then 1.5 mg/kg IV q8h)
s12.explain: The regimen is ampicillin 2 g IV every 6 hours + gentamicin 2 mg/kg IV load then 1.5 mg/kg IV every 8 hours, continued until delivery.

s12.q: When is clindamycin or metronidazole added to the chorioamnionitis antibiotic regimen?
options: Only if cesarean delivery is performed (for anaerobic coverage)
Only if vaginal delivery is performed
Only if the patient is penicillin-allergic
s12.explain: If cesarean delivery is performed, clindamycin or metronidazole is added for anaerobic coverage because surgical incision exposes deep pelvic tissues to anaerobic flora from the vagina.

s12.q: Which of the following is a key concept?
options: It is essential for understanding the condition
It is rarely relevant in clinical practice
It applies only to specific populations
It has been replaced by newer classifications
s12.explain: Understanding key concepts is fundamental to clinical management.

s12.q: The clinical significance of this section is:
options: It applies only to research settings
s12.explain: Understanding these concepts directly informs clinical management.
